summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the conventional wisdom of 'don't reinvent the wheel' by arguing that reimplementing existing solutions can be a valuable learning exercise. He cites examples from James Hart, Charles Moore (creator of FORTH), and Bob Lee (Android core library lead) who all advocate for the benefits of rewriting code yourself. Moore's practice of rewriting subroutines across 18 different CPUs made him exceptionally skilled and produced smaller, faster code than standard solutions. Atwood concludes that the adage should encourage deep study of existing solutions rather than serve as a weapon against legitimate attempts to improve or learn.
key_points: Categorically refusing to solve problems with code once any library exists is too extreme an interpretation of 'don't reinvent the wheel'
Charles Moore's practice of writing his own subroutines across dozens of implementations made him highly skilled and produced superior results
The phrase 'don't reinvent the wheel' is often used to discourage competition or avoid learning new things rather than as genuine advice
Deep study of existing solutions should be the real takeaway from this principle, not prohibition against building alternatives
Most programmers who write standard subroutines have done so only once, limiting their expertise compared to those who practice repeatedly
money_quotes: Don't Reinvent The Wheel, Unless You Plan on Learning More About Wheels
Most of our current technology sucks, and even if it didn't, who am I to try and stop you?
After writing the same subroutine a dozen times on as many computers and languages, you'll be pretty good at it.
You can almost always replace 'Why reinvent the wheel?' with 'Please don't compete with me,' or 'Please don't make me learn something new.'
He never re-used his own code without re-examining it for possible improvements.
It's a bit dangerous if taken too literally – if you categorically deny all attempts to solve a problem with code once any existing library is in place.
More seeds, more blooms, I say. Don't build houses on kitchen sinks. Reinvent away.
key_insight: The prohibition against reinventing the wheel should inspire deep study of existing solutions, not serve as a weapon against those who want to learn by building or legitimately improve on what exists.
